mnemonics: BUBBLE Sinks Slowly: Bubble Sort slowly sinks the largest element to the end with each pass, like a bubble rising to the surface. SELECT the Smallest: Selection Sort 'selects' the smallest element from the unsorted part and swaps it into place.
keyPoints: Selection Sort has a consistent O(n²) time complexity for all cases, making it inefficient for large datasets.
Bubble Sort is an in-place, stable sorting algorithm that is optimized if it detects a pass with no swaps.
Selection Sort performs a minimal number of memory writes (O(n)) compared to other simple sorts, but is generally not stable.
commonMistakes: Confusing the inner loop mechanisms: Bubble Sort compares adjacent elements and swaps, while Selection Sort finds the min/max index first and performs one swap per pass.
Assuming Bubble Sort is efficient. Its average and worst-case complexity is O(n²), making it impractical for most real-world use.
Forgetting that a basic implementation of Selection Sort is not stable, which can matter when sorting objects with equal keys.
